content:
# Ephesians — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Ephesians (Greek: Pros Ephesious) | **Chapters:** 6

## At a Glance
- **Author:** The letter claims to be from Paul (1:1, 3:1). However, Ephesians is among the "disputed" Pauline letters — a significant number of scholars consider it pseudonymous (written by a follower of Paul), based on vocabulary (over 40 words not found elsewhere in Paul), style (long, complex sentences — 1:3–14 is one sentence in Greek), theology (cosmic ecclesiology, realized eschatology), and literary relationship to Colossians (extensive verbal parallels).
- **Date:** If Pauline: c. 60–62 CE (during imprisonment). If pseudonymous: c. 80–90 CE.
- **Audience:** The words "in Ephesus" (1:1) are absent from the earliest manuscripts (P46, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). This suggests the letter may have been a circular letter to multiple churches, with the destination left blank for insertion.
- **Genre:** Letter — though its lack of personal greetings, specific situations, and local references makes it more like a theological treatise in letter form.
- **Timeline:** c. 60–62 CE or c. 80–90 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** Ephesians presents the most developed ecclesiology in the New Testament — the church as the body of Christ, the mystery of Jew-Gentile unity, and the cosmic scope of God's plan to "unite all things in Christ."

## The World Behind the Text
Ephesus was the largest city in Roman Asia Minor, home to the Temple of Artemis (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World). Paul spent approximately three years there (Acts 19:10, 20:31). The city has been extensively excavated — the theater (capacity 25,000, where the riot of Acts 19:29 is set), the library of Celsus, the agora, and inscriptions mentioning Asiarchs (Acts 19:31) and the temple warden (neōkoros, Acts 19:35) have been uncovered.

The absence of "in Ephesus" from the earliest manuscripts is the key textual fact. If the letter was not specifically addressed to Ephesus, it may have been a general letter — Marcion (c. 140 CE) apparently knew it as the letter "to the Laodiceans" (Tertullian, Against Marcion 5.17), possibly connecting it to the letter mentioned in Colossians 4:16.

## Why This Book Was Written
Ephesians develops two major themes: cosmic Christology and the church as Christ's body.

**1:3–14:** A single Greek sentence (202 words in the NA28 text) — a blessing describing God's plan "before the foundation of the world" (1:4) to unite all things in Christ (1:10 — anakephalaiōsasthai, "to sum up/bring together under one head").

**2:11–22:** The "dividing wall of hostility" (2:14) between Jew and Gentile has been broken down. Some scholars connect this to the literal dividing wall in the Jerusalem temple (the soreg) that separated the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts — a wall confirmed by the Soreg inscription (discovered 1871), which warns non-Jews of the death penalty for trespass.

**5:21–6:9:** The household code (Haustafel) — instructions for wives/husbands, children/parents, slaves/masters — follows a pattern found also in Colossians 3:18–4:1. These codes reflect Greco-Roman household management literature (Aristotle, Politics 1.1253b) adapted to Christian contexts.

## How to Read This Book
**1:1–3:21:** Theological — what God has done (blessing, grace, unity, mystery revealed). **4:1–6:20:** Ethical — how to live ("walk worthy," 4:1). The structure is similar to Romans (theology then ethics) but more compact.

The "armor of God" passage (6:10–17) — belt of truth, breastplate of righteousness, shoes of the gospel, shield of faith, helmet of salvation, sword of the Spirit — draws on Isaiah 11:5, 59:17 and the Wisdom of Solomon 5:17–20, where God puts on armor. Paul (or the author) transfers divine armor to believers.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
Ephesians and Colossians share approximately one-third of their vocabulary. Entire phrases are nearly identical (compare Ephesians 6:21–22 with Colossians 4:7–8). The literary relationship is evident but its direction (Colossians first? a common author? a later author using Colossians?) is debated.

Ephesians 5:31 quotes Genesis 2:24 ("a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh") and then adds: "This mystery is profound — but I am talking about Christ and the church" (5:32). This interpretive move — reading the Genesis marriage text as referring to Christ and the church — influenced centuries of Christian marriage theology and sacramental theology.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **The absence of "in Ephesus" from P46, Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus** is the most important textual variant. It affects the letter's genre — specific letter vs. circular epistle.
- **The Soreg inscription** ("No foreigner is to enter within the balustrade and forecourt around the sacred precinct. Whoever is caught will have himself to blame for his death") — found in Jerusalem — may illuminate "the dividing wall of hostility" (2:14), though this reading is debated.
- **1:3–14 as a single sentence** — this is not a translation artifact; the Greek is genuinely one continuous sentence of extraordinary theological density.
- **The household codes (5:21–6:9)** are the most controversial section — "Wives, submit to your husbands" (5:22) has generated more interpretive discussion than perhaps any other Pauline text. The code begins with mutual submission: "Submit to one another out of reverence for Christ" (5:21).
- **"Principalities and powers" (3:10, 6:12)** — Ephesians describes a cosmic conflict involving spiritual forces (archē, exousia, dynamis, kyriotēs). Whether these refer to angelic beings, political structures, spiritual realities, or all three, is debated.
- **P46 and P49 (3rd century)** provide the earliest manuscript evidence.

## Timeline
- **c. 52–55 CE:** Paul's Ephesian ministry (Acts 19)
- **c. 60–62 CE:** Traditional date (during imprisonment)
- **c. 80–90 CE:** Alternative date (if pseudonymous)
- **c. 140 CE:** Marcion knows the letter as "to the Laodiceans"
- **c. 200 CE:** P46 — earliest manuscript (without "in Ephesus")
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus (without "in Ephesus"), Codex Vaticanus

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
